title: Type Hints & Mypy
description: Python type hints from basics to Staff+ patterns: generics, Protocols, overload, TypedDict, type narrowing, variance, and mypy strict mode.

sections.title: 1. Basic Type Hints — Syntax
sections.description:
Type hints are not enforced at runtime (unless using pydantic). They're for static checkers (mypy, pyright) and documentation. Run mypy --strict for full enforcement.

Basic types: int, float, str, bool, None. Use list[int], dict[str, int], tuple[str, int], set[int] from typing (Python <3.9) or builtins (Python 3.9+).

Optional[X] is equivalent to Union[X, None]. Prefer X | None syntax in Python 3.10+.

FAANG angle: 'Why use type hints?' — catches bugs at static-check time (not runtime), serves as documentation with teeth, enables better IDE autocomplete, and is required at Google for production Python code.
sections.codeBlock:
from typing import Optional, Union, List, Dict, Tuple, Set, Any

# Python 3.9+ can use builtin generics
def process_users(
    ids: list[int],
    mapping: dict[str, int] | None = None,
    threshold: float = 0.5,
) -> list[str]:
    '''Filter users above threshold.'''
    if mapping is None:
        return [str(i) for i in ids]
    return [str(k) for k, v in mapping.items() if v > threshold]

# Optional vs Union vs |
def fetch(key: str) -> Optional[int]:  # equivalent to Union[int, None]
def fetch2(key: str) -> int | None:     # Python 3.10+ preferred

# Any unsets type checking — avoid
def unchecked(x: Any) -> Any:
    return x.some_attr  # no type error

sections.title: 2. Type Aliases, NewType, and Callable
sections.description:
Type aliases simplify complex types. NewType creates distinct types (different at static-check time, same at runtime). Callable[[Arg1, Arg2], ReturnType] types function signatures.

FAANG angle: 'How do you represent a user ID vs an order ID when both are strings?' — UserId = NewType('UserId', str). Mypy catches passing an order ID where a user ID is expected, but no runtime overhead.
sections.codeBlock:
from typing import Callable, NewType, TypeAlias

# Type alias
UserId = NewType('UserId', str)
OrderId = NewType('OrderId', str)

JSON: TypeAlias = dict[str, 'JSON'] | list['JSON'] | str | int | float | bool | None

# NewType catches cross-type errors at static-check time
def get_user(user_id: UserId) -> dict:
    return {'id': user_id}

uid = UserId('abc123')
get_user(uid)          # OK
# get_user('abc123')   # mypy error: str != UserId
# get_user(OrderId('xyz'))  # mypy error: OrderId != UserId

# Callable
transformer: Callable[[int], str] = str

# Higher-order function types
def apply(func: Callable[[int], int], val: int) -> int:
    return func(val)

print(apply(lambda x: x * 2, 5))  # 10

sections.title: 3. Generics — TypeVar and Constraints
sections.description:
TypeVar creates type parameters. Use bound to constrain to a class hierarchy. Use ParamSpec (PEP 612) for decorators that preserve signatures.

Invariance vs covariance: list[T] is invariant (list[Dog] is NOT a list[Animal]). Sequence[T] is covariant (you can read). Immutable containers are typically covariant; mutable ones are invariant.

FAANG angle: 'Implement a type-safe registry' — use TypeVar('T', bound=BaseModel) so the registry only accepts model subclasses.

sections.title: 4. Structural Subtyping — Protocols
sections.description:
Protocol (PEP 544) enables static duck typing. A class satisfies a protocol if it has the required methods — no inheritance needed. This is structural subtyping, vs ABC's nominal subtyping.

Use @runtime_checkable to use isinstance() at runtime.

Decision: ABC when you control the hierarchy (framework base classes). Protocol when you don't (accepting third-party objects that happen to have the right methods).

FAANG angle: 'Design a serializer that accepts any object with .to_dict()' — define a Serializable(Protocol) with def to_dict(self) -> dict: ....

sections.title: 5. Overload, TypedDict, and Literal
sections.description:
@overload: multiple type signatures for one function. The body must handle all cases. TypedDict: typed dictionary with specific keys. Literal: exact value constraints.

FAANG angle: 'Type a function that returns str when the input is str and int when it's int' — use @overload.

sections.title: 6. Staff+ Patterns — Variance, ParamSpec, and mypy Strict
sections.description:
Variance: A Producer[T] (reads T) should be covariant. A Consumer[T] (writes T) should be contravariant. A Mutator[T] (both) must be invariant. Python's list is invariant for this reason.

ParamSpec (PEP 612) preserves decorator signatures. Concatenate adds parameters.

mypy strict mode (mypy --strict) enables all optional checks: disallow_untyped_defs, warn_return_any, warn_unused_ignores, strict_equality.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design a decorator that logs function calls and preserves types' — use ParamSpec + TypeVar. At Google, typed decorators are the baseline expectation for production Python.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• mypy docs — Optional static typing
  • typing module — Official reference
  • PEP 484 — Type Hints
  • PEP 544 — Protocols
  • PEP 612 — ParamSpec
  • Python Typing Playground — pyright-play.net

details:
Typing workflow: (1) Start with mypy --strict on new projects. (2) Add types to existing code incrementally. (3) Use Protocol for structural typing (looser coupling). (4) Use ParamSpec for decorators (preserve signatures). (5) Run mypy in CI as a blocking check.

Common mistakes: Using Any everywhere (defeats type checking); not constraining TypeVar when needed; using list when Sequence (read-only) is sufficient; ignoring mypy with # type: ignore without a reason comment; not running --strict.

Staff+ expectations: Design type-safe abstractions for the team. Know when to use Protocol vs ABC vs duck typing. Write overloaded signatures for complex APIs. Ensure CI enforces typing as strictly as linting. Teach the team about variance and ParamSpec.
